title: Building the Innovative Organization & Open Innovation
simple_html:
Innovation is not just a flash of genius from one founder — it is something an organization can be designed to produce again and again. This lesson is about the conditions, culture, and structures that make some companies reliably innovative, and about open innovation: the idea that good ideas also come from outside the company.

What makes an organization innovative

Innovative firms tend to share a few traits: a clear vision and leadership support for new ideas, tolerance of smart failure, the freedom ("slack") for people to experiment, cross-functional teamwork, and rewards for creativity. Culture matters as much as budgets — if people fear punishment for failed experiments, they stop trying.

The ambidexterity challenge

A big firm must do two opposite things at once: exploit its current business efficiently (cut costs, perfect today's product) and explore new possibilities (risky, slow, uncertain). Doing both is called being ambidextrous. Often this means separate teams or units so the disciplined core does not crush the experimental edge.

Intrapreneurship

Employees who act like entrepreneurs inside a company are intrapreneurs. Firms support them with dedicated time, internal funding, incubators, and "skunkworks" teams kept apart from bureaucracy.

Open innovation

Henry Chesbrough coined open innovation: the idea that firms should use external ideas and paths to market as much as internal ones. Instead of inventing everything in-house ("closed" innovation), companies license technology in and out, partner with startups, run crowdsourcing contests, and co-create with customers.

Key takeaways:

  • Innovation can be built into structure and culture, not left to luck.
  • Balance exploiting today's business with exploring tomorrow's (ambidexterity).
  • Encourage intrapreneurs with slack, funding, and protection from bureaucracy.
  • Open innovation taps ideas from outside; not all smart people work for you.
intermediate_html:
This lesson covers how to design an organization that innovates consistently and how to manage the flow of ideas across the firm's boundary. It combines the internal view (culture, structure, ambidexterity, intrapreneurship) with the external view (open innovation modes and ecosystems).

Components of the innovative organization

Research on innovative firms (Tidd & Bessant) points to recurring enablers: a shared vision and committed leadership; an appropriate structure (often flat and organic rather than rigidly mechanistic); key individuals such as champions and gatekeepers; high-involvement teamwork; a creative climate that tolerates failure and rewards experimentation; and strong external focus on customers and partners. The point is that innovation capability is a system, not a single lever.

Exploitation vs exploration and ambidexterity

March (1991) distinguished exploitation (refining, efficiency, certainty, short-term returns) from exploration (search, variation, experimentation, long-term, uncertain). Both are necessary but compete for resources and require different cultures. Organizational ambidexterity is the capacity to do both. Two main approaches:

  • Structural ambidexterity — separate units for exploit and explore, each with its own processes, integrated only at the top (e.g., a dedicated new-ventures division).
  • Contextual ambidexterity — individuals split their own time between the two within one unit, enabled by a supportive context (stretch + discipline + trust).

Intrapreneurship / corporate venturing

To capture exploration inside an incumbent, firms use intrapreneurship and corporate venturing mechanisms: dedicated slack time (e.g., Google's 20%), internal venture funds, incubators/accelerators, skunkworks (autonomous teams shielded from the core), and corporate venture capital investing in external startups. The recurring tension is autonomy versus integration — too separate and the venture cannot leverage the parent; too integrated and it gets strangled by core-business metrics.

Open innovation

Chesbrough (2003) contrasted closed innovation (do-it-all-internally, hoard IP) with open innovation: "the use of purposive inflows and outflows of knowledge to accelerate internal innovation and expand the markets for external use of innovation." Three modes:

  1. Outside-in (inbound) — bring external ideas/technology in: licensing-in, scouting, crowdsourcing, university partnerships, acquiring startups.
  2. Inside-out (outbound) — let unused ideas exit to others: licensing-out, spin-offs, donating IP to a platform.
  3. Coupled — co-create through alliances, joint ventures, and ecosystems.

Worked example

Procter & Gamble's Connect + Develop program is the textbook case. Facing slowing internal R&D productivity, P&G set a goal that 50% of innovations should involve an external partner. Through technology scouting, supplier networks, and open challenges it in-licensed ideas (e.g., the Pringles printed-chip technology came from an external bakery) and dramatically raised R&D efficiency. This illustrates outside-in open innovation tied explicitly to the firm's business model.

How to apply it

Diagnose your firm on two axes. First, the ambidexterity gap: are you over-investing in exploitation and starving exploration? If so, create a structurally separate explore unit with different metrics. Second, the openness gap: which parts of your value chain could draw on external knowledge, and which dormant internal IP could be licensed out for revenue? Crucially, open innovation requires absorptive capacity — the firm's ability to recognize, assimilate, and apply external knowledge — so investment in internal R&D remains necessary to absorb outside ideas. This links to appropriability (how you capture value) and to the financing and ecosystem lessons.
full_html:
The romantic image of the lone inventor obscures a managerial reality: sustained innovation is an organizational capability that can be deliberately built and, increasingly, sourced across the firm's boundary. This lesson synthesizes the internal architecture of the innovative organization (culture, structure, ambidexterity, intrapreneurship) with Chesbrough's open-innovation paradigm and its ecosystem implications, and connects both to absorptive capacity and value appropriation.

The anatomy of the innovative organization

Tidd and Bessant (Managing Innovation) distil decades of research into a set of components of the innovative organization: a clear and shared vision/leadership, an appropriate (organic) structure, key individuals (champions, sponsors, gatekeepers), effective team working, high-involvement innovation across all staff, a creative climate, and a strong external orientation. The classic structural insight traces to Burns and Stalker (1961), who distinguished mechanistic structures (hierarchical, formalized — suited to stable environments) from organic structures (fluid, lateral, suited to dynamic, innovative environments). Amabile's (1996) componential theory adds that creativity at work depends on expertise, creative-thinking skills, and — most malleable by management — intrinsic motivation, which a controlling climate destroys.

Exploration, exploitation, and ambidexterity

March (1991) framed the central trade-off: organizations must balance exploration (search, variation, risk-taking, experimentation) against exploitation (refinement, efficiency, execution). Exploitation yields reliable near-term returns and tends to crowd out exploration because its payoffs are more certain and proximate — the root of the competency trap and of why successful incumbents fail at radical change. Tushman and O'Reilly (1996) proposed structural ambidexterity: physically separate, differently-cultured units for explore and exploit, reconciled by a common strategic intent and senior-team integration. Gibson and Birkinshaw (2004) advanced contextual ambidexterity: building an organizational context (combining performance management with social support) that lets individuals divide their attention between alignment and adaptability. Both relate to Christensen's (1997) disruption logic — incumbents should house disruptive ventures in autonomous units freed from the parent's resource-allocation and customer-listening processes.

Intrapreneurship and corporate venturing

Pinchot (1985) popularized intrapreneuring — entrepreneurial behavior inside established firms. Burgelman's (1983) field studies of internal corporate venturing show innovation often emerges bottom-up through autonomous strategic behavior that later reshapes the firm's strategy. Mechanisms span a spectrum of autonomy: slack/bootleg time, idea markets, internal incubators and accelerators, skunkworks (Lockheed's archetype), new-venture divisions, and corporate venture capital (CVC) for external bets. The governing dilemma is the autonomy-integration tension: ventures need independence from the core's metrics and culture to grow, yet must eventually leverage the parent's complementary assets to scale. Mismanaging this is why many corporate ventures either get smothered by core-business KPIs or remain orphaned and starved.

Open innovation

Chesbrough (2003) named the paradigm shift from closed to open innovation. The closed model — generate, develop, and commercialize internally, protecting IP — was undermined by greater labor mobility, abundant venture capital, and capable suppliers, all of which made knowledge leak across boundaries. Open innovation is "the use of purposive inflows and outflows of knowledge to accelerate internal innovation and expand the markets for external use of innovation" (Chesbrough, 2006). Three archetypal modes:

ModeDirectionExamples
Outside-in (inbound)External knowledge inIn-licensing, scouting, crowdsourcing, university ties, startup acquisition
Inside-out (outbound)Internal knowledge outOut-licensing, spin-offs, technology donation, platform opening
CoupledBidirectional co-creationAlliances, JVs, consortia, innovation ecosystems

Critically, openness does not eliminate the need for internal R&D; Cohen and Levinthal's (1990) concept of absorptive capacity — "the ability to recognize the value of new external information, assimilate it, and apply it" — means firms must invest internally to exploit external knowledge. Laursen and Salter (2006) further showed an inverted-U relationship between search breadth/depth and performance: openness pays, but over-search (too many external sources) yields diminishing and even negative returns due to attention and integration costs.

Appropriating value from open and ecosystem innovation

Openness raises the question of how the firm captures value. Teece's (1986) profiting-from-innovation (PFI) framework is the anchor: returns depend on the strength of the appropriability regime (IP protection, tacitness) and ownership of complementary assets (manufacturing, distribution, brand, ecosystem). When appropriability is weak and complementary assets are specialized and tightly held by others, the innovator may lose to imitators or asset-holders — a reason to combine open inbound sourcing with control of key complements. This dovetails with platform and ecosystem strategy and with the network-effects and dominant-design lessons.

Worked example: P&G Connect + Develop

Confronting declining R&D productivity around 2000, P&G's Lafley set a target that half of innovations originate with external partners. Using technology scouting, supplier networks, and open problem broadcasting (e.g., InnoCentive-style challenges), P&G sourced products such as the Olay Regenerist active and the Pringles Prints technology (adapted from an Italian bakery's ink-jet method). Results widely reported include R&D productivity rising roughly 60% and innovation success rates more than doubling. The case demonstrates open innovation working only because P&G retained strong internal absorptive capacity and brand/distribution complementary assets to commercialize what it sourced.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Treating culture as soft/optional. A controlling, failure-punishing climate kills intrinsic motivation (Amabile) regardless of R&D spend.
  • Forcing one structure to do both jobs. Asking the efficient core to also explore usually means exploration loses; separate or contextually enable it.
  • "Open" = free or costless. Open innovation requires investment in absorptive capacity and partner governance; naive crowdsourcing without integration capacity fails.
  • Over-searching. More external sources is not always better (Laursen & Salter's inverted-U).
  • Ignoring value capture. Sourcing ideas openly without an appropriability/complementary-asset strategy lets others capture the rents (Teece).
  • Smothering ventures with core KPIs. Intrapreneurial units judged by mainstream financial metrics rarely survive their uncertain early phase.

Cross-links

This lesson builds on Foundations (Lesson 1) and Types of Innovation & Disruption (Lesson 2 — why incumbents struggle with disruptive change), and feeds into Financing & Launching New Ventures (Lesson 8 — CVC and venture funding as external financing). Its appropriability and ecosystem themes also reinforce the dominant-design and network-effects material.
